---
title: "Episode 2 — Version Control with Git"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Objectives
By the end of this episode you will be able to:

- Initialize a repository and record snapshots with meaningful commits.
- Read history and inspect what changed.
- Use branches to develop a feature safely.
- Collaborate via a remote and a pull request.

**Teaching:** 40 min · **Exercises:** 25 min
:::

## Why version control?

Robotics configs and code change constantly — and a bad change can crash hardware. Git lets
you record every change, understand who changed what and why, experiment on branches, and
collaborate without overwriting each other. It is also the backbone of CI/CD (Episode on
pipelines) and reproducible research.

## Starting a repo (live coding)

```bash
mkdir nav-tuning && cd nav-tuning
git init
git status
```

Configure your identity once (per machine):

```bash
git config --global user.name  "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"
```

## The add → commit cycle

```bash
echo "max_speed: 1.5" > nav.yaml
git status                       # untracked
git add nav.yaml                 # stage
git commit -m "Add initial nav config"
git log --oneline
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Instructor note
Draw the three areas — working directory → staging area → repository — on screen or in the
notes. The staging area is the most common point of confusion; revisit it after the first
challenge.
:::

Make a change and see the diff:

```bash
echo "max_accel: 0.8" >> nav.yaml
git diff                         # what changed since last commit
git add nav.yaml
git commit -m "Add acceleration limit"
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge 1 — Tell the story
Write a one-line commit that would help a teammate six months from now. Which is better, and
why?

- `git commit -m "fix"`
- `git commit -m "Lower max_speed to 1.0 after lab collision on 2026-06-20"`

### Solution
The second. Good commit messages explain *why*, not just *what*. Future-you and your
teammates read history to understand decisions, especially after an incident.
:::

## Branching for safe experiments

Never tune a live robot on `main`. Branch:

```bash
git switch -c faster-turns
echo "max_turn_rate: 2.0" >> nav.yaml
git commit -am "Increase turn rate for tight corridors"
git switch main                  # main is untouched
git log --oneline --graph --all
```

Merge when you're happy:

```bash
git merge faster-turns
```

## Collaborating with a remote

```bash
# After creating an empty repo on GitHub:
git remote add origin https://github.com/you/nav-tuning.git
git push -u origin main
```

The collaborative workflow: **fork → branch → commit → push → pull request → review → merge.**
This is exactly how you'll contribute to ForgeLab lessons (see
[CONTRIBUTING](https://github.com/FrankAsanteVanLaarhoven/ForgeLabCarpentries/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md)).

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge 2 — Recover from a mistake
You edited `nav.yaml` with a bad value but haven't committed. How do you discard the change and
get back the last committed version?

### Solution

```bash
git restore nav.yaml      # discard unstaged changes to this file
```
If you already staged it: `git restore --staged nav.yaml` first, then `git restore nav.yaml`.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Key points
- Commit small, logical changes with messages that explain *why*.
- `git diff` and `git log` let you read history and changes.
- Branches isolate risky work — keep `main` deployable.
- The fork/PR workflow is how teams collaborate and how CI gets triggered.
:::

Next: [Episode 3 — Robot navigation with ROS 2](03-ros2-navigation.qmd).
